blocks.title: The thesis
paragraphs: VIA Labs operates two complementary businesses under one roof. VIA Labs is the agentic commerce protocol: a software platform that brands and merchants pay to make themselves discoverable and transactable by AI agents. RealReal Genuine (RRG) is the first live product running on the platform, and the reference implementation of VIA's full stack.

The financial picture below treats them as separate P&Ls then consolidates. RRG's role is strategic as well as commercial: it is the proof point that attracts other brand merchants onto VIA Labs, and it functions as an onboarding funnel. RRG is intentionally managed close to break-even; upside lives in VIA Labs once merchant scale kicks in from 2028.

blocks.title: Agent-first operating team
paragraphs: VIA Labs is being built as an agent-first company. This is not an aspirational statement about a future org chart — it describes how the team is already structured today. Across every operational area of the business — product development, research, marketing, customer service, sales support, operations, admin, and content — the default mode of execution is an agent with a human in the loop, not a human with tooling. Humans are added only where an agent cannot yet discharge the function reliably, and the bar for adding a human is rising over time, not falling.

This principle is what makes the cost structure below credible at scale. The Customer Success and Leadership lines that appear from 2027–2028 are therefore best read as the minimum human layer required to run a venture-backed company at this revenue size, not as a conventional SaaS org chart. Every role named in the leadership block exists because the function is presently beyond what agents can own end-to-end; every role that does not appear — in-house HR, General Counsel, tier-1 support headcount, content production staff, junior engineers, research analysts — is a deliberate call to cover that function agent-first from day one.

The practical implication for anyone reading this plan is that the revenue-per-FTE ratios will look unusual by SaaS norms, and the operating leverage in the business does not come from classic economies of scale but from compounding automation. As the agent layer gets better, either the same human team supports materially more revenue, or that human team shrinks on a per-dollar-of-revenue basis. The plan below is deliberately sized against the current capability of the agent stack; improvements to that stack represent unmodelled upside, not base-case assumption.

blocks.title: Revenue timing
paragraphs: Material revenue starts in Q4 2026. The seed round closes mid-Q2 2026, leaving a 3 to 6 month build window before paid usage begins. 2026 numbers should therefore be read as a partial year; the full revenue model is exercised from 2027 onward.

VIA Labs revenue has three components: recurring subscription (growth / professional / enterprise tiers), platform fees charged per intent, quote, and transaction, and a margin on agent 'top-up' wallet funding. Subscription gives a predictable floor; platform fees scale with actual usage; the top-up margin scales with GMV once agents transact autonomously.

blocks.title: RRG commission model
paragraphs: RRG takes a two-tier platform fee keyed on product type rather than drop format. Digital products (priced up to $10) carry a 30% take rate — this bucket covers co-created digital drops, digital-only brand items, and any sub-$10 digital SKU. Physical products (above $10) carry a flat 2.5% take rate across the full price range, with no further tiering. This structure is deliberately simpler than the earlier four-tier model: it matches how merchants actually think about the split (digital vs physical) and it removes a pricing cliff in the $10–$100 band.

The blended rate therefore starts relatively high (digital is a larger share of GMV early, while the brand catalogue is still being built out) and compresses as higher-priced physical product takes share. Commission revenue is complemented by a direct co-creation revenue line: essentially RRG's share of drops it originates itself.

blocks.title: Cost discipline
paragraphs: Both businesses are run lean. VIA Labs headcount stays founder-plus-contractor through 2027; DL (CPO / Co-founder) joins at $6k/month from May 2026. An advisory board (SR, MS, TM, DW, PC, AF) receives $10k/year each, paid half-yearly. From 2028, a full leadership layer is brought online alongside a dedicated Customer Success function (see the stress-test note below). Distribution is driven by product quality, the RRG lighthouse, and developer-relations investment in agentic commerce standards, complemented from 2028 by a Head of BD / Enterprise Sales focused on the largest merchants.

RRG costs are dominated by design, brand partnerships, and marketing: the line items you'd expect of a drop-led fashion business. Fulfilment is deliberately not carried: RRG uses the co-creating brand's own warehouse and logistics, so that cost bucket sits at zero.

blocks.title: Cash
paragraphs: Starting cash is the $5M seed raise. Combined EBITDA is negative in 2026 and 2027 and turns positive in 2028. Cash trough is ~$3.8M end of 2027; the business never requires a bridge round under the baseline assumptions. By 2030, cumulative EBITDA exceeds $100M on consolidated revenue near $95M (with VIA Labs the dominant contributor).

blocks.title: Sensitivities
paragraphs: The most sensitive input is Active Merchants on the VIA Labs side: it drives subscription, platform fees, and (via GMV) agent top-up margin. The conversion funnel (intents to quotes to transactions) is conservative in 2026 and steps up each year; readers should stress-test the conversion curve rather than the take rates, since the latter are anchored in the deployed smart contracts.

On the RRG side, the headline variable is total GMV: platform commission and co-creation both scale against it. Drop-mix assumptions are informed by the brand's 20-year history of limited-edition, collaboration-led releases.

blocks.title: LLM, hosting & infrastructure assumptions
paragraphs: The AI/LLM API, cloud hosting, database and blockchain gas lines in the P&L are sized against current list prices from the providers we use today (DeepSeek, Claude, Vercel, Supabase, Base / Optimism L2). Two opposing forces will move this envelope over the plan period.

Downward pressure: model inference pricing has fallen roughly 10x per year since 2023, hosting has moved decisively to usage-based pricing with steep volume discounts, and L2 gas costs continue to compress. Our base case assumes a conservative fraction of that trajectory, and we expect per-unit costs to decline materially before 2030.

Upward pressure: as VIA develops new tools, services and agent capabilities, anticipated LLM usage per merchant will rise — richer intents, multi-step quoting, autonomous negotiation, tool-use chains, and analytics all consume more tokens per interaction than the current baseline. The net effect is modelled to be broadly flat-to-favourable in aggregate, but the composition of this line will shift from 'volume of simple calls' to 'fewer, more capable calls' over time.

blocks.title: Stress-tested projections
paragraphs: The projections shown here have been stress-tested against the operational volumes they imply. By 2030 the model assumes roughly 540 million intents/year, 65 million transactions/year, and $8.4 billion of platform GMV flowing through VIA Labs. The cost base has been sanity-checked against real provider pricing at that scale.

As a result of that stress test, two new cost categories have been added to the VIA Labs P&L that sit alongside the original structure. Customer Success ramps from zero in 2026 to ~$900k/year by 2030, covering a lean CS team, enterprise customer success managers for the top tier, and tooling (Zendesk/Intercom-class). Even in an agent-first support model, Pro and Enterprise merchants require human touch points, and we have budgeted for them explicitly rather than hiding them inside G&A.

Leadership brings a proper executive layer online on a staggered ramp rather than a single step. A fractional CFO joins in 2027 at $60k/year and steps up to $120k/year from 2028. The Head of Partnerships starts in Q1 2027, and the Head of BD / Enterprise Sales and Head of Engineering both join in mid-2027. The COO joins in 2028. CEO and CPO compensation step up to $200k each from 2028 to reflect the operating reality of a company at this revenue scale. External legal retainers are also increased by 50% from 2028 onward, in lieu of hiring a General Counsel in-house.

The combined effect of these additions reduces 2030 EBITDA by roughly 3–4% versus the pre-stress-test model. Margin compresses from ~87% to ~85%; the consolidated business remains deeply cash-generative and does not require a follow-on raise under the baseline. The business model is robust to these adjustments; the point of adding the lines is to make the plan defensible, not to change its shape.

Items deliberately not added after stress-testing: in-house General Counsel (covered via external legal), dedicated HR / People lead (absorbed by leadership team through 2030), and a dedicated operations / warehouse function on the RRG side (brand partners retain fulfilment).
